---
title: "ProCSI-C Training"
subtitle: "Two-Week Summer Camp, Summer 2026"
author: "Dan Negrut"
date: 2026-07-13
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    width: 1250
    margin: 0.08
    transition: fade
    footer: "ProCSI-C Training  |  UW-Madison | CSU System"
    embed-resources: true
    controls: true
    controls-layout: bottom-right
    controls-tutorial: false
---

## Acknowledgments {.smaller}

- The ProCSI-C training is a collaboration between the University of Wisconsin-Madison (UW-Madison) and the California State University System (CSU).
    - ProCSI-C: Promoting the Computational Science Initiative (for College students)
    - There have been many ProCSI-H events at UW-Madison for **H**igh school students.

\

- ProCSI-C is supported by the National Science Foundation (NSF) under grant OAC-2519443.

\

- **Khailanii Slaton** has been a great help with the lab tours and the overall organization of the camp.

## How the camp works {.smaller}

\

- Each **morning**, a short brief: today's topic, your target, and where to start. Then good luck.
    - We meet each morning at 9:30 am in this room (1153ME)

\

- The lab tours in the College of Engineering are scattered throughout the week, so we will do them on different days and at different times. But the times will be known at the beginning of the camp.

\

- You work in **teams**, hands-on, all day (i.e., whenever you do not have lunch or lab tours). **Claude is your always-on tutor.**

## How the camp works: afternoons & more {.smaller}

- Late afternoon, from **4:00 to 5:00**, each team gives a friendly **~10-minute** talk: how far you got and what you learned.
    - Begin every talk with your team's **quote of the day**.
    - Length of the talk: 10 minutes. Strictly enforced.
    - Use the projector in the meeting room along with your laptop.


## How the camp works: afternoons & more {.smaller}

- **No grades, no quizzes.** Discover new things, make new friends, and enjoy a little friendly competition.

\

- All our meetings and work sessions will be in room 1153ME in the Mechanical Engineering building at UW-Madison.

\

- No food or drinks are allowed in the room.


## Teams {.smaller}
- Each day we'll have five fresh teams, each made up of **four** people.

\

- We pick **5 captains**. Captains take turns (round-robin) drafting *free agents* into **their own teams**.
    - No captain can be a captain twice *during one week*.
    - Role of the captains: they present their team's work at the end of the day.

\

- **New teams each day**, so you keep meeting new people.
    
\

- Teams compete good-naturedly; at day's end, everyone **votes for a favorite** summary of the day.
    - The only stakes are bragging rights and fun.

## If things are too easy for you

- You are part of a team, but you move faster than the rest of the team.
- If you want to quickly cover the entire material, do so.
- Let Dan know when you are done with covering the material that is assigned for the entire program.
- Based on your interests, Dan will work with you to identify a side quest for sim-in-robotics to keep you busy
 
 \

- You will **still** have to be part of a team, on a daily basis.
    - But you will be able to work on other things in "solo" mode.


## Before you start

- Bring along your own **laptop** (at least one good one per team).
- Each team member needs a free **Onshape** account: it runs in the browser, nothing to install.
- We install **Miniforge (conda)** and **PyChrono** together, no prior setup needed.
- Curiosity required; experience not.

## Accounts to create (all free)

- **Onshape** ([onshape.com](https://www.onshape.com)): CAD in the browser; every team member needs one.
- **Claude** ([claude.ai](https://claude.ai)): your AI tutor for the whole camp.
- **GitHub** ([github.com](https://github.com)): optional; only if you take the git side quest.

## Software to install {.smallest}

| Tool | When | Windows | Linux | macOS |
|------|------|---------|-------|-------|
| git | Day 2 | installer from [git-scm.com](https://git-scm.com); includes **Git Bash**, the bash we use | `sudo apt install git` (often already there) | type `git --version` in Terminal, accept the popup |
| Blender | Days 4, 5, 7 | installer from [blender.org](https://www.blender.org) | `sudo snap install blender --classic` (or your distro's package) | installer from [blender.org](https://www.blender.org) |
| Miniforge (conda + Python) | Day 3 on; **we install it together** | [github.com/conda-forge/miniforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge) | same link | same link |
| PyChrono | Week 2; **we install it together** | nothing to do now | nothing to do now | nothing to do now |

\

- git is only a fetch tool here. \
The one command you will need:
  `git clone https://github.com/DanNegrut/procsi-c-training.git`

# Week 1

## Week 1 at a glance

| Day | Focus |
|-----|-------|
| Mon, Jul 13 | Welcome, College of Engineering, meet everyone |
| Tue, Jul 14 | Command line interface (CLI) and bash; meet Claude |
| Wed, Jul 15 | Python (conda) |
| Thu, Jul 16 | Blender: render your first image |
| Fri, Jul 17 | Onshape mechanism, then render it in Blender |

## Monday, July 13

- CSULA cohort arriving late
- [PM, 1153ME]{.pm} How the camp works; getting to know each other
    - Dan answers your questions
- [PM]{.pm} Ice cream expedition at Memorial Union; walk there after dinner

## Tuesday, July 14

- [AM]{.am} The command line interface (CLI) and bash: files, folders, navigation; `git clone` as just another command -- learn what this command does
- [AM]{.am} Meet Claude, your tutor for the whole camp
- [PM]{.pm} Hands-on in teams; ~10-minute talks and the first quote of the day

## Wednesday, July 15

- [AM]{.am} Install Miniforge (conda); Python basics: variables, loops, functions, arrays
- [AM]{.am} Optional side quest, for the curious: what git and GitHub actually are. The camp itself only needs the one `git clone` command from yesterday.
- [PM]{.pm} Python exercises with Claude; team talks

## Thursday, July 16

- [AM]{.am} Blender basics; render a still image
- [PM]{.pm} Teams render a scene; team talks
- [PM]{.tour} Lab tour

## Friday, July 17

- [AM]{.am} Onshape: build a simple mechanism (slider-crank or four-bar); export
- [PM]{.pm} Import the CAD part into Blender, set a scene, render; team talks

\

- **NOTE**: Group picture at **4:00 PM**, Mechanical Engineering atrium.

# Week 2

## Week 2 at a glance {.smaller}

\

| Day | Focus |
|-----|-------|
| Mon, Jul 20 | What does "simulate" mean? The sliding brick and forward Euler numerical integration |
| Tue, Jul 21 | Turn the math into a Blender movie of the brick |
| Wed, Jul 22 | PyChrono: the same brick for real, plus wow demos |
| Thu, Jul 23 | AI-assisted modeling with RAG (chrono-rag) |
| Fri, Jul 24 | State Street send-off; pack up |

## Monday, July 20

- [AM]{.am} The sliding brick: mass m, force f, over 10 s; the equation of motion; forward Euler
- [PM]{.pm} Teams code the Euler loop in Python and plot position vs time; team talks

## Tuesday, July 21

- [AM]{.am} From numbers to motion: turn the Euler output (a CSV) into a Blender keyframe animation
- [PM]{.pm} Teams render their brick movie over a real backdrop; team talks

## Wednesday, July 22

- [AM]{.am} Install PyChrono (conda); the **same brick**, now with real contact and friction
- [AM]{.am} Two demos: a slider-crank mechanism, and a rover on terrain
- [PM]{.tour} Lab tour
- [PM]{.pm} Teams run and tweak a demo; team talks

## Thursday, July 23

- [AM]{.am} What is RAG? Use chrono-rag  to generate a PyChrono model, then run it
- [PM]{.pm} Bigger models, generated with chrono-rag, or picked up from existing repositories; team talks

## Friday, July 24

- [AM]{.am} Head to State Street and purchase swag; help the Madison economy.
- [PM]{.pm} Pack up; get ready for Saturday departure
